techniques.evidence_quote: targeting AWS accounts and most of the time caused by a leaked IAM user access key
Ran malicious containers in these clusters, by creating ECS task definitions... Scaled each task definition using an ECS service, to ensure that each task definition runs 25 tasks
created a new administrator IAM user with a console profile... the attacker created two additional administrator IAM users
created two additional administrator IAM users, along with a login profile and an access key
eventName: SendSSHPublicKey... sSHPublicKey: ssh-ed25519 [AWS autogenerated key]
the attacker attempted to run EC2 instances in an unused region, us-west-1
ec2:AuthorizeSecurityGroupIngress — Open security group to 0.0.0.0/0 on port 22

they used the console to enumerate a number of elements such as EC2 instances running in the account, volumes, security groups, and load balancers. They also leveraged the AWS Resource Explorer to list resources
the attacker manually clicked on several S3 buckets to view objects inside them, browsed their directory tree... generating ListObjects / HeadObject events
the attacker attempted to access several EC2 instances through EC2 Instance Connect... eventName: SendSSHPublicKey
downloaded files, generating ListObjects / HeadObject / GetObject events as they went
downloads XMRig and starts mining cryptocurrency... ./$APP -a rx/0 -o stratum+tcp://randomxmonero.auto.nicehash.com:9200
